Aubert argues that law is a structural element in the Aeneid, in that the
foundation of Rome is understood both as a material phenomenon,
involving organization of the urban space, and as an ideal phenomenon,
involving regulation of the civitas. Curtis's book includes chapters on "Virgil's
Aeneid and the Relocation of Ritual" and "Foundational Choreography in
the Aeneid." Fowler's 1997 contribution on Vergilian narrative is revised
and updated by Barchiesi. Haudry discerns Indo-European motifs in the
Aeneid. Tar suggests that an analysis of allegorical interpretations of myths
from antiquity to the present shows Vergil to be a creator, not merely a
manipulator, of myths. Thomas, observing that the place of the sublime in
the Roman imaginary deserves to be recognized, finds that the characters in
the Aeneid who participate in the sublime are found in two registers: that of
tragedy and that of epic.

Boldrer defends the authenticity of Servius's testimony at Ecl. 6.11 for
a meeting between Cicero and Vergil on the occasion of the theatrical
performance of that eclogue. Deloince-Louette compares the views of the
French humanist Jean de Sponde (1557-95), the Spanish theologian Juan
Vergilian Bibliography - 179
Luis de la Cerda (1558-1643), and the French poet Jacques Peletier (1517-
82) concerning Vergil's relationship to Homer. Foster's focus is late Roman
education and the ways in which students from different cultural and
religious backgrounds were trained to read Vergil's multifaceted narrative.
Fowler's 1997 contribution on Servius's commentary is revised and updated
by Casali and Stok. Guillaumin offers an edition, translation into French,
and commentary on Servius, Aen. 4. Peraki-Kyriakidou offers an analysis of
Servius's translation practice and his use of the term translatio, which can
refer to metaphor and to etymology. Ramires is concerned with Servius's
interpretation of Aen. 4.30.
